**Metrology** is the scientific study of [measurement](/wiki/Measurement "Measurement").[[1]](#cite_note-BIPM-1) It establishes a common understanding of [units](/wiki/Unit_of_measurement "Unit of measurement"), crucial in linking human activities.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2) Modern metrology has its roots in the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution")'s political motivation to standardise units in France when a length standard taken from a natural source was proposed. This led to the creation of the decimal-based [metric system](/wiki/Metric_system "Metric system") in 1795, establishing a set of standards for other types of measurements. Several other countries adopted the metric system between 1795 and 1875; to ensure conformity between the countries, the *[Bureau International des Poids et Mesures](/wiki/International_Bureau_of_Weights_and_Measures "International Bureau of Weights and Measures")* (BIPM) was established by the [Metre Convention](/wiki/Metre_Convention "Metre Convention").[[3]](#cite_note-BGtoM-3)[[4]](#cite_note-French-History-4) This has evolved into the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI) as a result of a resolution at the 11th [General Conference on Weights and Measures](/wiki/General_Conference_on_Weights_and_Measures "General Conference on Weights and Measures") (CGPM) in 1960.[[5]](#cite_note-R12_11-5)

Metrology is divided into three basic overlapping activities:[[6]](#cite_note-C-S-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

These overlapping activities are used in varying degrees by the three basic sub-fields of metrology:[[6]](#cite_note-C-S-6)

In each country, a national measurement system (NMS) exists as a network of laboratories, [calibration](/wiki/Calibration "Calibration") facilities and accreditation bodies which implement and maintain its metrology infrastructure.[[8]](#cite_note-National_Measurement_System-8)[[9]](#cite_note-NQI-9) The NMS affects how measurements are made in a country and their recognition by the international community, which has a wide-ranging impact in its society (including economics, energy, environment, health, manufacturing, industry and consumer confidence).[[10]](#cite_note-MSC-10)[[11]](#cite_note-AusEconomy-11) The effects of metrology on trade and economy are some of the easiest-observed societal impacts. To facilitate fair trade, there must be an agreed-upon system of measurement.[[11]](#cite_note-AusEconomy-11)

## History

The ability to measure alone is insufficient; standardisation is crucial for measurements to be meaningful.[[12]](#cite_note-MSC-History-12) The first record of a permanent standard was in 2900 BC, when the [royal Egyptian cubit](/wiki/Cubit#Ancient_Egyptian_royal_cubit "Cubit") was carved from black [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite").[[12]](#cite_note-MSC-History-12) The cubit was decreed to be the length of the Pharaoh's forearm plus the width of his hand, and replica standards were given to builders.[[3]](#cite_note-BGtoM-3) The success of a standardised length for the building of [the pyramids](/wiki/Giza_pyramid_complex "Giza pyramid complex") is indicated by the lengths of their bases differing by no more than 0.05 percent.[[12]](#cite_note-MSC-History-12)

In China weights and measures had a semi religious meaning as it was used in the various crafts by the [Artificers](/wiki/Kao_Gong_Ji "Kao Gong Ji") and in ritual utensils and is mentioned in the [book of rites](/wiki/Book_of_Rites "Book of Rites") along with the [steelyard balance](/wiki/Steelyard_balance "Steelyard balance") and other tools.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Other civilizations produced generally accepted measurement standards, with Roman and Greek architecture based on distinct systems of measurement.[[12]](#cite_note-MSC-History-12) The collapse of the empires and the Dark Ages that followed lost much measurement knowledge and [standardisation](/wiki/Standardisation "Standardisation"). Although local systems of measurement were common, comparability was difficult since many local systems were incompatible.[[12]](#cite_note-MSC-History-12) England established the Assize of Measures to create standards for length measurements in 1196, and the 1215 [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") included a section for the measurement of wine and beer.[[14]](#cite_note-NPL-HofL-14)

Modern metrology has its roots in the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). With a political motivation to harmonise units throughout France, a length standard based on a natural source was proposed.[[12]](#cite_note-MSC-History-12) In March 1791, the [metre](/wiki/Metre "Metre") was defined.[[4]](#cite_note-French-History-4) This led to the creation of the decimal-based [metric system](/wiki/Metric_system "Metric system") in 1795, establishing standards for other types of measurements. Several other countries adopted the metric system between 1795 and 1875; to ensure international conformity, the [International Bureau of Weights and Measures](/wiki/International_Bureau_of_Weights_and_Measures "International Bureau of Weights and Measures") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Bureau International des Poids et Mesures*, or BIPM) was formed by the [Metre Convention](/wiki/Metre_Convention "Metre Convention").[[3]](#cite_note-BGtoM-3)[[4]](#cite_note-French-History-4) Although the BIPM's original mission was to create international standards for units of measurement and relate them to national standards to ensure conformity, its scope has broadened to include electrical and [photometric](/wiki/Photometry_(optics) "Photometry (optics)") units and [ionizing radiation](/wiki/Ionizing_radiation "Ionizing radiation") measurement standards.[[4]](#cite_note-French-History-4) The metric system was modernised in 1960 with the creation of the [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI) as a result of a resolution at the 11th [General Conference on Weights and Measures](/wiki/General_Conference_on_Weights_and_Measures "General Conference on Weights and Measures") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Conference Generale des Poids et Mesures*, or CGPM).[[5]](#cite_note-R12_11-5)

## Subfields

Metrology is defined by the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM) as "the science of measurement, embracing both experimental and theoretical determinations at any level of uncertainty in any field of science and technology".[[15]](#cite_note-WhatIsBIPM-15) It establishes a common understanding of units, crucial to human activity.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2) Metrology is a wide reaching field, but can be summarized through three basic activities: the definition of internationally accepted units of measurement, the realisation of these units of measurement in practice, and the application of chains of traceability (linking measurements to reference standards).[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2)[[6]](#cite_note-C-S-6) These concepts apply in different degrees to metrology's three main fields: scientific metrology; applied, technical or industrial metrology, and legal metrology.[[6]](#cite_note-C-S-6)

### Scientific metrology

Scientific metrology is concerned with the establishment of units of measurement, the development of new measurement methods, the realisation of measurement standards, and the transfer of traceability from these standards to users in a society.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2)[[3]](#cite_note-BGtoM-3) This type of metrology is considered the top level of metrology which strives for the highest degree of accuracy.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2) BIPM maintains a database of the metrological calibration and measurement capabilities of institutes around the world. These institutes, whose activities are peer-reviewed, provide the fundamental reference points for metrological traceability. In the area of measurement, BIPM has identified nine metrology areas, which are acoustics, electricity and magnetism, length, mass and related quantities, photometry and radiometry, ionizing radiation, time and frequency, thermometry, and chemistry.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

As of May 2019 no physical objects define the base units.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The motivation in the change of the base units is to make the entire system derivable from [physical constants](/wiki/Physical_constants "Physical constants"), which required the removal of the prototype kilogram as it is the last artefact the unit definitions depend on.[[18]](#cite_note-SD-18) Scientific metrology plays an important role in this redefinition of the units as precise measurements of the physical constants is required to have accurate definitions of the base units. To redefine the value of a kilogram without an artefact the value of the [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant") must be known to twenty parts per billion.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Scientific metrology, through the development of the [Kibble balance](/wiki/Kibble_balance "Kibble balance") and the [Avogadro project](/wiki/Avogadro_project "Avogadro project"), has produced a value of Planck constant with low enough uncertainty to allow for a redefinition of the kilogram.[[18]](#cite_note-SD-18)

### Applied, technical or industrial metrology

Applied, technical or industrial metrology is concerned with the application of measurement to manufacturing and other processes and their use in society, ensuring the suitability of measurement instruments, their calibration and quality control.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2) Producing good measurements is important in industry as it has an impact on the value and quality of the end product, and a 10–15% impact on production costs.[[6]](#cite_note-C-S-6) Although the emphasis in this area of metrology is on the measurements themselves, traceability of the measuring-[device](/wiki/Measuring_instrument "Measuring instrument") calibration is necessary to ensure confidence in the measurement. Recognition of the metrological competence in industry can be achieved through mutual recognition agreements, accreditation, or peer review.[[6]](#cite_note-C-S-6) Industrial metrology is important to a country's economic and industrial development, and the condition of a country's industrial-metrology program can indicate its economic status.[[20]](#cite_note-silva-20)

### Legal metrology

Legal metrology "concerns activities which result from statutory requirements and concern measurement, [units of measurement](/wiki/Unit_of_measurement "Unit of measurement"), measuring instruments and methods of measurement and which are performed by competent bodies".[[21]](#cite_note-VIML-21) Such statutory requirements may arise from the need for protection of health, public safety, the environment, enabling taxation, protection of consumers and fair trade. The International Organization for Legal Metrology ([OIML](/wiki/OIML "OIML")) was established to assist in harmonising regulations across national boundaries to ensure that legal requirements do not inhibit trade.[[22]](#cite_note-DeWayne-22) This harmonisation ensures that certification of measuring devices in one country is compatible with another country's certification process, allowing the trade of the measuring devices and the products that rely on them. [WELMEC](/wiki/WELMEC "WELMEC") was established in 1990 to promote cooperation in the field of legal metrology in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and among [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (EFTA) member states.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In the United States legal metrology is under the authority of the Office of Weights and Measures of [National Institute of Standards and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology") (NIST), enforced by the individual states.[[22]](#cite_note-DeWayne-22)

## Concepts

### Definition of units

The [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI) defines seven base units: [length](/wiki/Length "Length"), [mass](/wiki/Mass "Mass"), [time](/wiki/Time "Time"), [electric current](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current"), [thermodynamic temperature](/wiki/Thermodynamic_temperature "Thermodynamic temperature"), [amount of substance](/wiki/Amount_of_substance "Amount of substance"), and [luminous intensity](/wiki/Luminous_intensity "Luminous intensity").[[24]](#cite_note-NIST_SI-24) By convention, each of these units are considered to be mutually independent and can be constructed directly from their defining constants.[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 129 All other SI units are constructed as products of powers of the seven base units.[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 129

SI base units and standards

| Base quantity | Name | Symbol | Definition |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Time | [second](/wiki/Second "Second") | s | The duration of 9192631770 periods of the radiation corresponding to the transition between the two [hyperfine](/wiki/Hyperfine_structure "Hyperfine structure") levels of the [ground state](/wiki/Ground_state "Ground state") of the [caesium-133](/wiki/Isotopes_of_caesium#Caesium-133 "Isotopes of caesium") atom[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 130 |
| Length | [metre](/wiki/Metre "Metre") | m | The length of the path travelled by light in a [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum") during a time interval of 1/299792458 of a second[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 131 |
| Mass | [kilogram](/wiki/Kilogram "Kilogram") | kg | Defined ([as of 2019](/wiki/2019_revision_of_the_SI "2019 revision of the SI")) by "... taking the fixed numerical value of the [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant"), *h*, to be 6.62607015×10−34 when expressed in the unit J s, which is equal to kg m2 s−1 ..."[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 131 |
| Electric current | [ampere](/wiki/Ampere "Ampere") | A | Defined (as of 2019) by "... taking the fixed numerical value of the [elementary charge](/wiki/Elementary_charge "Elementary charge"), *e*, to be 1.602176634×10−19 when expressed in the unit C, which is equal to A s ..."[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 132 |
| Thermodynamic temperature | [kelvin](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin") | K | Defined (as of 2019) by "... taking the fixed numerical value of the [Boltzmann constant](/wiki/Boltzmann_constant "Boltzmann constant"), *k*, to be 1.380649×10−23 when expressed in the unit J K−1, which is equal to kg m2 s−2 K−1 ..."[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 133 |
| Amount of substance | [mole](/wiki/Mole_(unit) "Mole (unit)") | mol | Contains (as of 2019) "... exactly 6.02214076×1023 elementary entities. This number is the fixed numerical value of the [Avogadro constant](/wiki/Avogadro_constant "Avogadro constant"), *N*A, when expressed in the unit mol−1 ..."[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 134 |
| Luminous intensity | [candela](/wiki/Candela "Candela") | cd | The luminous intensity, in a given direction, of a source emitting monochromatic radiation of a frequency of 540×1012 Hz with a radiant intensity in that direction of 1/683 watt per [steradian](/wiki/Steradian "Steradian")[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 135 |

Since the base units are the reference points for all measurements taken in SI units, if the reference value changed all prior measurements would be incorrect. Before 2019, if a piece of the international prototype of the kilogram had been snapped off, it would have still been defined as a kilogram; all previous measured values of a kilogram would be heavier.[[3]](#cite_note-BGtoM-3) The importance of reproducible SI units has led the BIPM to complete the task of defining all SI base units in terms of [physical constants](/wiki/Physical_constant "Physical constant").[[26]](#cite_note-redef-26)

By defining SI base units with respect to physical constants, and not artefacts or specific substances, they are realisable with a higher level of precision and reproducibility.[[26]](#cite_note-redef-26) As of the revision of the SI on 20 May 2019 the [kilogram](/wiki/Kilogram "Kilogram"), [ampere](/wiki/Ampere "Ampere"), [kelvin](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin"), and [mole](/wiki/Mole_(unit) "Mole (unit)") are defined by setting exact numerical values for the [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant") (*h*), the [elementary electric charge](/wiki/Elementary_electric_charge "Elementary electric charge") (*e*), the [Boltzmann constant](/wiki/Boltzmann_constant "Boltzmann constant") (*k*), and the [Avogadro constant](/wiki/Avogadro_constant "Avogadro constant") (*N*A), respectively. The [second](/wiki/Second "Second"), [metre](/wiki/Metre "Metre"), and [candela](/wiki/Candela "Candela") have previously been defined by physical constants (the [caesium standard](/wiki/Caesium_standard "Caesium standard") (Δ*ν*Cs), the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light") (*c*), and the [luminous efficacy](/wiki/Luminous_efficacy "Luminous efficacy") of 540×1012 Hz visible light radiation (*K*cd)), subject to correction to their present definitions. The new definitions aim to improve the SI without changing the size of any units, thus ensuring continuity with existing measurements.[[27]](#cite_note-Kuehne-27)[[25]](#cite_note-SI_9th_edition-25): 123, 128

### Realisation of units

![Computer-generated image of a small cylinder](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/CGKilogram.jpg/250px-CGKilogram.jpg)

The [realisation](/wiki/Realisation_(metrology) "Realisation (metrology)") of a unit of measure is its conversion into reality.[[28]](#cite_note-OED-28) Three possible methods of realisation are defined by the [international vocabulary of metrology](/wiki/Joint_Committee_for_Guides_in_Metrology#VIM:_International_vocabulary_of_metrology "Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology") (VIM): a physical realisation of the unit from its definition, a highly-reproducible measurement as a reproduction of the definition (such as the [quantum Hall effect](/wiki/Quantum_Hall_effect "Quantum Hall effect") for the [ohm](/wiki/Ohm "Ohm")), and the use of a material object as the measurement standard.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Standards

A [standard](/wiki/Standard_(metrology) "Standard (metrology)") (or etalon) is an object, system, or experiment with a defined relationship to a unit of measurement of a physical quantity.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Standards are the fundamental reference for a system of weights and measures by realising, preserving, or reproducing a unit against which measuring devices can be compared.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2) There are three levels of standards in the hierarchy of metrology: primary, secondary, and working standards.[[20]](#cite_note-silva-20) Primary standards (the highest quality) do not reference any other standards. Secondary standards are calibrated with reference to a primary standard. Working standards, used to calibrate (or check) measuring instruments or other material measures, are calibrated with respect to secondary standards. The hierarchy preserves the quality of the higher standards.[[20]](#cite_note-silva-20) An example of a standard would be [gauge blocks](/wiki/Gauge_blocks "Gauge blocks") for length. A gauge block is a block of metal or ceramic with two opposing faces ground precisely flat and parallel, a precise distance apart.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The length of the path of light in vacuum during a time interval of 1/299,792,458 of a second is embodied in an artefact standard such as a gauge block; this gauge block is then a primary standard which can be used to calibrate secondary standards through mechanical comparators.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Traceability and calibration

![Pyramid illustrating the relationship between traceability and calibration](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Traceability_Pyramid.png/500px-Traceability_Pyramid.png)

Metrological traceability is defined as the "property of a measurement result whereby the result can be related to a reference through a documented unbroken chain of calibrations, each contributing to the measurement uncertainty".[[33]](#cite_note-VIM-33) It permits the comparison of measurements, whether the result is compared to the previous result in the same laboratory, a measurement result a year ago, or to the result of a measurement performed anywhere else in the world.[[34]](#cite_note-WMO-34) The chain of traceability allows any measurement to be referenced to higher levels of measurements back to the original definition of the unit.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2)

Traceability is obtained directly through [calibration](/wiki/Calibration "Calibration"), establishing the relationship between an indication on a standard traceable measuring instrument and the value of the comparator (or comparative measuring instrument). The process will determine the measurement value and uncertainty of the device that is being calibrated (the comparator) and create a traceability link to the measurement standard.[[33]](#cite_note-VIM-33) The four primary reasons for calibrations are to provide traceability, to ensure that the instrument (or standard) is consistent with other measurements, to determine accuracy, and to establish reliability.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2) Traceability works as a pyramid, at the top level there is the international standards, which beholds the world's standards. The next level is the national Metrology institutes that have primary standards that are traceable to the international standards. The national Metrology institutes standards are used to establish a traceable link to local laboratory standards, these laboratory standards are then used to establish a traceable link to industry and testing laboratories. Through these subsequent calibrations between national metrology institutes, calibration laboratories, and industry and testing laboratories the realisation of the unit definition is propagated down through the pyramid.[[34]](#cite_note-WMO-34) The traceability chain works upwards from the bottom of the pyramid, where measurements done by industry and testing laboratories can be directly related to the unit definition at the top through the traceability chain created by calibration.[[3]](#cite_note-BGtoM-3)

### Uncertainty

[Measurement uncertainty](/wiki/Measurement_uncertainty "Measurement uncertainty") is a value associated with a measurement which expresses the spread of possible values associated with the [measurand](/wiki/Measurand "Measurand")—a quantitative expression of the doubt existing in the measurement.[[35]](#cite_note-EUROLAB-35) There are two components to the uncertainty of a measurement: the width of the uncertainty interval and the confidence level.[[36]](#cite_note-BGtoU-36) The uncertainty interval is a range of values that the measurement value expected to fall within, while the confidence level is how likely the true value is to fall within the uncertainty interval. Uncertainty is generally expressed as follows:[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2)

![{\displaystyle Y=y\pm U}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/aa7166924115bca144fcf32dfa93cdbf072048d0)

Where *y* is the measurement value and *U* is the uncertainty value and *k* is the coverage factor[[a]](#cite_note-37) indicates the confidence interval. The upper and lower limit of the uncertainty interval can be determined by adding and subtracting the uncertainty value from the measurement value. The coverage factor of *k* = 2 generally indicates a 95% confidence that the measured value will fall inside the uncertainty interval.[[2]](#cite_note-FCM-2) Other values of *k* can be used to indicate a greater or lower confidence on the interval, for example *k* = 1 and *k* = 3 generally indicate 66% and 99.7% confidence respectively.[[36]](#cite_note-BGtoU-36) The uncertainty value is determined through a combination of statistical analysis of the calibration and uncertainty contribution from other errors in measurement process, which can be evaluated from sources such as the instrument history, manufacturer's specifications, or published information.[[36]](#cite_note-BGtoU-36)

## International infrastructure

Several international organizations maintain and standardise metrology.

### Metre Convention

The [Metre Convention](/wiki/Metre_Convention "Metre Convention") created three main [international organizations](/wiki/International_organization "International organization") to facilitate standardisation of weights and measures. The first, the General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM), provided a forum for representatives of member states. The second, the International Committee for Weights and Measures (CIPM), was an advisory committee of metrologists of high standing. The third, the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM), provided secretarial and laboratory facilities for the CGPM and CIPM.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

#### General Conference on Weights and Measures

The [General Conference on Weights and Measures](/wiki/General_Conference_on_Weights_and_Measures "General Conference on Weights and Measures") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Conférence générale des poids et mesures*, or CGPM) is the convention's principal decision-making body, consisting of delegates from member states and non-voting observers from associate states.[[38]](#cite_note-39) The conference usually meets every four to six years to receive and discuss a CIPM report and endorse new developments in the SI as advised by the CIPM. The last meeting was held on 13–16 November 2018. On the last day of this conference there was vote on the redefinition of four base units, which the [International Committee for Weights and Measures](/wiki/International_Committee_for_Weights_and_Measures "International Committee for Weights and Measures") (CIPM) had proposed earlier that year.[[39]](#cite_note-cipm_106-40) The new definitions came into force on 20 May 2019.[[40]](#cite_note-SI-statement-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)

#### International Committee for Weights and Measures

The [International Committee for Weights and Measures](/wiki/International_Committee_for_Weights_and_Measures "International Committee for Weights and Measures") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Comité international des poids et mesures*, or CIPM) is made up of eighteen (originally fourteen)[[42]](#cite_note-43) individuals from a member state of high scientific standing, nominated by the CGPM to advise the CGPM on administrative and technical matters. It is responsible for ten consultative committees (CCs), each of which investigates a different aspect of metrology; one CC discusses the measurement of temperature, another the measurement of mass, and so forth. The CIPM meets annually in [Sèvres](/wiki/S%C3%A8vres "Sèvres") to discuss reports from the CCs, to submit an annual report to the governments of member states concerning the administration and finances of the BIPM and to advise the CGPM on technical matters as needed. Each member of the CIPM is from a different member state, with France (in recognition of its role in establishing the convention) always having one seat.[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)

#### International Bureau of Weights and Measures

![BIPM seal: three women, one holding a measuring stick](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Metric_seal.svg/250px-Metric_seal.svg.png)

The [International Bureau of Weights and Measures](/wiki/International_Bureau_of_Weights_and_Measures "International Bureau of Weights and Measures") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Bureau international des poids et mesures*, or BIPM) is an organisation based in Sèvres, France which has custody of the [international prototype of the kilogram](/wiki/International_prototype_of_the_kilogram "International prototype of the kilogram"), provides metrology services for the CGPM and CIPM, houses the secretariat for the organisations and hosts their meetings.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-IPK-47) Over the years, prototypes of the metre and of the kilogram have been returned to BIPM headquarters for recalibration.[[46]](#cite_note-IPK-47) The BIPM director is an [ex officio member](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member") of the CIPM and a member of all consultative committees.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### International Organization of Legal Metrology

The [International Organization of Legal Metrology](/wiki/International_Organization_of_Legal_Metrology "International Organization of Legal Metrology") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Organisation Internationale de Métrologie Légale*, or OIML), is an [intergovernmental organization](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organization "Intergovernmental organization") created in 1955 to promote the global harmonisation of the legal metrology procedures facilitating international trade.[[48]](#cite_note-49) This harmonisation of technical requirements, test procedures and test-report formats ensure confidence in measurements for trade and reduces the costs of discrepancies and measurement duplication.[[49]](#cite_note-strategy-50) The OIML publishes a number of international reports in four categories:[[49]](#cite_note-strategy-50)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Metrology's history spans from ancient body-based measures, like the Egyptian cubit, to today's precision instruments, showcasing humanity's quest for consistent and accurate measurements.

Significant milestones include the establishment of the metric system during the French Revolution and the 1960 introduction of the International System of Units (SI), standardizing global measurements.

Technological advancements, including atomic clocks and quantum metrology, have revolutionized measurement precision, enabling applications in diverse fields such as scientific research and industrial processes.
